blocks.text: This is what the Lord God showed me: he was forming locusts at the time the latter growth began to sprout (it was the latter growth after the king's mowings).
1 : A locust plague at the time of endangered the spring planting, just sprouting, after the harvest of the winter crops.
When they had finished eating the grass of the land, I said, “O Lord God, forgive, I beg you! How can Jacob stand? He is so small!”
2 : The prophet appeals to God's sense of proportion: Israel ( ) is and, by implication, God is so big (cf. Job 7.20).
The Lord relented concerning this; “It shall not be,” said the Lord.
7.1–3: Judgment by locusts (cf. Joel 1.2–7).
3 : , God's mind changed concerning the punishment, although forgiveness is not offered, as Amos requested (see also Ex 32.14; Joel 2.13; Jon 4.2).
This is what the Lord God showed me: the Lord God was calling for a shower of fire, and it devoured the great deep and was eating up the land.
4 : , the cosmic waters under the earth and over the heavens (see Gen 1.2).
Then I said, “O Lord God, cease, I beg you! How can Jacob stand? He is so small!”
The Lord relented concerning this; “This also shall not be,” said the Lord God.
7.4–6: Judgment by fire.
This is what he showed me: the Lord was standing beside a wall built with a plumb line, with a plumb line in his hand.
And the Lord said to me, “Amos, what do you see?” And I said, “A plumb line.” Then the Lord said, “See, I am setting a plumb line in the midst of my people Israel; I will never again pass them by;
the high places of Isaac shall be made desolate, and the sanctuaries of Israel shall be laid waste, and I will rise against the house of Jeroboam with the sword.”

7.1–9: The first three of five visions and Amos's intercession. Amos acts as an intercessor on behalf of Israel (see Gen 18.17; Ex 32.7–14). Yet prophetic intercession, while effective in the short term, will ultimately not deter God from punishing sinful Israel.

7.7–9: Israel falls short. The (2 Kings 21.13–15), a device for determining the true vertical line of a structure, reveals that Israel's religious and political institutions do not measure up and will be destroyed; here the prophet offers no intercessory protest or plea, though he does maintain a dialogue with God. were open-air Though acceptable in earlier eras, Amos and Hosea (10.8) condemned them as places of illicit worship. , a rare designation for the Northern Kingdom (see v. 16). , the royal dynasty of Jeroboam II (788–747 ).
Then Amaziah, the priest of Bethel, sent to King Jeroboam of Israel, saying, “Amos has conspired against you in the very center of the house of Israel; the land is not able to bear all his words.
10 : Whether or not the charge against Amos is accurate, prophets such as Elisha did engage in political upheavals (2 Kings 9.1–10).
For thus Amos has said, ‘Jeroboam shall die by the sword, and Israel must go into exile away from his land.’”

11 : , Amaziah does not attribute the prophet's words to God. The first utterance Amaziah cites ( …) is the rough but not exact equivalent of 7.9; the second phrase ( …) is similar to 4.2–3; 5.5,26–27; 6.7; 9.4.
And Amaziah said to Amos, “O seer, go, flee away to the land of Judah, earn your bread there, and prophesy there;
12 : , lit., “eat bread.”
but never again prophesy at Bethel, for it is the king's sanctuary, and it is a temple of the kingdom.”
Then Amos answered Amaziah, “I am no prophet, nor a prophet's son; but I amb a herdsman, and a dresser of sycamore trees,

14 : Amos asserts that he is neither a professional prophet (1 Sam 9.6–10; Mic 3.5–8,11) nor a member of a prophetic guild (2 Kings 2.3; 1 Sam 10.5; 1 Kings 22.6), but a of cattle (cf. 1.1n.) and seasonally a (see Introduction).
and the Lord took me from following the flock, and the Lord said to me, ‘Go, prophesy to my people Israel.’
“Now therefore hear the word of the Lord. You say, ‘Do not prophesy against Israel, and do not preach against the house of Isaac.’

Therefore thus says the Lord: ‘Your wife shall become a prostitute in the city, and your sons and your daughters shall fall by the sword, and your land shall be parceled out by line; you yourself shall die in an unclean land, and Israel shall surely go into exile away from its land.’”

7.1–9.15: The visions of Amos. The final section of the book consists of accounts and interpretations of five visions which become increasingly severe (7.1–3,4–6,7–9; 8.1–3; 9.1–6), a biographical account about the prophet (7.10–17), and prophetic oracles (8.4–14; 9.5–15).

7.10–17: Amos and Amaziah. This episode, a narrative illustration of 2.12 in the form of a dramatic encounter between the royal priest and the prophet, the classic outsider, is here perhaps because the reference to (v. 10) picks up the phrase “house of Jeroboam” (v. 9). Amaziah was the official priest of the royal shrine at Bethel (see 3.14n.).
17 : A dreadful string of curses: May … may others acquire title to your land (i.e., it with a measuring ) , Amos closes his response to Amaziah by repeating the latter's words from v. 11.
